narrative_blocks.header: Harvard Medical School, then Harpers Ferry campaign, 1862
narrative_blocks.body: Wilburn Whitley attended Harvard Medical School in 1855-56, trained as a physician, then served in the 62nd Pennsylvania Militia during the Harpers Ferry campaign of September 1862 — the same campaign in which his future brother-in-law Giles Hooker Cowles was also serving in a different regiment. He died at forty-four in Conneautville in 1870, outliving his first wife Caroline Edna Cowles by eleven years.

cross_connections.display_label: Both at Harpers Ferry, September 1862 — different regiments

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard Medical School, then Harpers Ferry campaign, 1862
narrative_blocks.body: Wilburn Whitley attended Harvard Medical School in 1855-56, trained as a physician, then served in the 62nd Pennsylvania Militia during the Harpers Ferry campaign of September 1862 — the same campaign in which his future brother-in-law Giles Hooker Cowles was also serving in a different regiment. He died at forty-four in Conneautville in 1870, outliving his first wife Caroline Edna Cowles by eleven years.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-seven; daughter raised by stepmother
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline Edna Cowles Whitley died July 12, 1859 at Conneautville Pennsylvania at twenty-seven, leaving her daughter Carriel just six years old in the care of Caroline's husband Dr. Wilburn Whitley. When Whitley himself died in 1870, stepmother Fidelia raised Carriel — the orphan of a twice-bereaved household.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband followed her in death eleven years later
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Wilburn Whitley, whom Caroline left a widower by her death at twenty-seven, survived her by eleven years — dying himself at forty-four in 1870, leaving daughter Carriel to be raised by stepmother Fidelia. The child outlived both parents and died in Conneautville at thirty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Died five years before her brother was killed
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline Edna Cowles Whitley died July 12, 1859 — five years before her younger brother Giles Hooker Cowles was killed in action before Petersburg on June 17, 1864. Their mother Lydia outlived them both, and their sister Cornelia Rachel would live until 1932.

sources.short: Cowles Families in America #689 — William Elbert Cowles

cross_connections.display_label: Her uncle Giles killed at Petersburg, 1864

narrative_blocks.header: Died two days before her thirty-second birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Known as Carrie, she lost her mother Caroline at age six and her father Dr. Wilburn Whitley at twelve — raised thereafter by stepmother Fidelia. A music teacher in Conneautville, she died there March 25, 1885, two days before what would have been her thirty-second birthday.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother dead at six; father at twelve
narrative_blocks.body: Carriel lost her mother Caroline Edna at age six when Caroline died at twenty-seven in 1859, and her father Dr. Wilburn Whitley at age twelve when he died in 1870, leaving stepmother Fidelia to raise her. She became a music teacher — known as Carrie — in Conneautville, and died there at thirty-one, two days before her thirty-second birthday.

burialCemetery.name: Conneautville Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Conneautville Cemetery, Conneautville Crawford County PA. Burial site of John Clarence Ellis (HD4274, gen 8) and likely multiple Ellis descendants. Located in northwestern Crawford County, north of Meadville.
